hook: Medieval dye-pits worked the same way for a thousand years, viewed from a leather shop's roof.
facts: Largest of the city's tanneries, in Fes el-Bali near the Saffarin Madrasa beside the Oued Fes.
Tradition ties its founding to Idris II in the 9th century; the firm record is murkier.
Stone vats hold lime, pigeon droppings and natural dyes; hides are softened, then coloured by foot.
Surrounding rooftops give the famous terrace views, sprigs of mint handed out against the stench.
legend: Local lore dates the pits to the city's foundation under Idris II.
w: Climb through a leather shop, mint sprig pressed to your nose against the reek, and look down on a honeycomb of stone vats where men stand thigh-deep dyeing hides by foot. Lime, pigeon droppings, saffron, poppy. Tradition dates it to Idris II. A thousand years, barely changed.

hook: Founded by a woman in 859, often called the oldest university still running, with a library only reopened in 2016.
facts: Founded in 859 by Fatima al-Fihri, daughter of a wealthy migrant from Kairouan, Tunisia.
UNESCO and Guinness call it the oldest continuously operating higher-education institution.
Its library holds manuscripts including a 9th-century Quran on camel skin and a copy of Ibn Khaldun.
Architect Aziza Chaouni led a restoration; the library reopened to the public in 2016.
w: Fatima al-Fihri spent her inheritance founding this in 859, and scholars have argued under its arches ever since. Often named the oldest university on earth. Non-Muslims glimpse only doorways, but the library, sealed for years and reopened in 2016 by architect Aziza Chaouni, guards a camel-skin Quran. Learning, older than nations.

hook: A Marinid sultan's college so grand it doubled as a Friday mosque, all cedar and zellij.
facts: Built 1350 to 1355 by the Marinid sultan Abu Inan Faris on Tala'a Kebira street.
The only madrasa in Fez to hold the status of a congregational Friday mosque.
Walls of carved cedar, stucco and zellij tile climb above a marble courtyard.
Open to non-Muslim visitors, unlike most active mosques in the medina.
w: Sultan Abu Inan poured a fortune into this college-mosque between 1350 and 1355, and when told it had cost too much reportedly threw the accounts in the river. Carved cedar, honeycomb stucco, a marble court. The rare madrasa where a non-Muslim may step inside. Marinid ambition, set in stone.

hook: A 14th-century hydraulic clock whose mechanism nobody alive fully understands.
facts: Stands opposite Bou Inania on Tala'a Kebira; reportedly completed in 1357.
Thirteen windows once opened in turn as a brass bowl caught a dropped metal ball each hour.
Engineered by al-Tlemsani, a mathematician and timekeeper, to mark the prayer hours.
Defunct for generations; lost documentation has defeated attempts to restore it.
legend: Some say a jinn turned the mechanism, since no one could explain its workings.
w: Thirteen weathered window-frames and a row of brass bowls, high on a wall facing Bou Inania. Built around 1357, this clock once dropped a metal ball each hour to mark prayer. It stopped generations ago and no one fully grasps how it ran. They say a jinn worked the gears. A puzzle, ticking no more.

hook: The perfumers' college, a jewel-box of zellij tucked behind the spice souk.
facts: Built 1323 to 1325 by the Marinid sultan Abu Said Uthman II.
Named for the neighbouring Souk al-Attarine, the spice and perfume market.
Small in footprint but lavished with carved stucco, cedar and dense mosaic tile.
Lodged students of the nearby al-Qarawiyyin for centuries.
w: Slip past the spice and perfume stalls and a plain door opens onto one of the medina's finest small courtyards. Built in the 1320s for students of al-Qarawiyyin, it crams carved cedar, stucco lace and tilework into a space you could cross in a few strides. Named for the perfumers next door. Marinid craft, distilled.

hook: A 1711 caravanserai where merchants once stabled mules, now a museum of woodwork beside a tiled fountain.
facts: Built in 1711 as a funduq, an inn with a courtyard for storing and selling goods.
Served briefly as a French colonial police station in the 1940s.
Reopened in 1998 as the Nejjarine Museum of Wooden Arts and Crafts.
The adjoining mosaic fountain dates to the 19th-century reign of Moulay Abd al-Rahman.
w: Caravans once unloaded here, mules below, traders sleeping above their bales. Built in 1711, the carpenters' funduq became a colonial police post, then a museum of doors, chests and instruments in cedar and thuya. The mosaic fountain has slaked throats for two centuries. A roof terrace pays you back with the medina laid bare.

hook: A sultan's Hispano-Moorish palace turned museum, its Andalusian garden grown wild and shady.
facts: Built 1886 to 1907 for the Alawi sultans Hassan I and Abdelaziz.
Converted to a museum of arts and crafts in 1915; collection now over 6,500 objects.
Famous for blue-and-white Fassi ceramics and carved architectural fragments.
Its garden was laid out by a French landscape architect around 1915, planted with jacaranda and palm.
w: Two sultans built this palace between 1886 and 1907, then it passed to the public as a museum in 1915. Inside, cobalt Fassi pottery and salvaged carved doors; outside, a walled Andalusian garden where a colossal Barbary oak and jacarandas throw shade over the dust. The city's blue and white, gathered under one roof.

hook: A 16th-century Saadian fort modelled on Portuguese bastions, now stuffed with five centuries of weaponry.
facts: Built in 1582 by the Saadi dynasty, echoing European gunpowder-age fort design.
Star-shaped corner bastions were likely added later under the Alaouites.
Houses the Museum of Arms, founded 1963, Morocco's first dedicated to arms and armour.
Collection of up to 5,000 pieces from 35 countries, prehistory to the 20th century.
w: A squat Saadian fort thrown up in 1582 to keep the unruly city in check, its cannon trained downhill on the medina rather than at any foreign foe. Inside now: yataghans, muskets, a five-metre siege gun, weapons from thirty-five countries. From its walls the whole tangle of Fes el-Bali spreads below. Power, pointed inward.

hook: Ruined royal mausolea on a bare hill, the city's best sunset and a dynasty's forgotten dead.
facts: A 14th-century royal necropolis of the Marinid dynasty, north of Fes el-Bali.
Two tall rectangular mausoleums with horseshoe-arch entrances still stand in ruin.
As of recent decades no thorough archaeological excavation had been carried out.
Now the favoured panoramic lookout over the old city, busiest at dusk.
w: Climb the bald northern hill at dusk and the whole medina smoulders gold below, the call to prayer rolling up from a thousand minarets. Behind you, two crumbling Marinid mausolea, horseshoe arches open to the wind, the sultans inside long unnamed and unexcavated. A ruined necropolis with the finest seat in Fez.

hook: A 17th-century synagogue behind an unmarked door, its rabbis drawn from one family for 150 years.
facts: Dated to around 1680, built by the wealthy merchant Mimon Boussidan.
Stands against the outer wall of the Mellah, Morocco's first Jewish quarter, set up in 1438.
Its rabbis came from the Ibn Danan family from 1812 until closure in the 1960s.
Restored and reopened in 1999; among the oldest surviving synagogues in Morocco.
w: A door no different from its neighbours, then stairs dropping into a tall painted room: carved wood ark, coloured glass, a sunken basin where the ritual bath once filled. Built around 1680, served by the Ibn Danan rabbis until the 1960s. The congregation has largely gone to Israel and France. Memory, kept behind a plain door.

hook: A field of blinding whitewashed tombs sloping toward the river, including a teenage martyr's grave.
facts: Moved to the Mellah's edge in 1894 when the Sultan expanded the Royal Palace.
Thousands of half-cylindrical whitewashed tombs, repainted bright white in 2019.
Holds the tomb of Rabbi Yehuda Ben Attar, born 1655.
A blue-painted grave marks Solica (Sol Hachuel), beheaded in 1834 at seventeen.
legend: Pilgrims venerate Solica as a martyr who chose death over forced conversion.
w: Rows on rows of low whitewashed tombs, repainted dazzling in 2019, tipping down toward the river like sun-bleached loaves. The old cemetery was shifted here in 1894 to make room for the palace. One blue grave holds Solica, a Tangier girl beheaded at seventeen in 1834; pilgrims still leave candles. The white city of the dead.

hook: The famous blue gate, only a century old, blue on one face and green on the other.
facts: Built by the French colonial administration in 1913 as a grand medina entrance.
Replaced an older nearby gateway that had carried the Bou Jeloud name for centuries.
Faced in cobalt zellij outside, the colour of Fez pottery; green inside, for Islam.
Frames the minarets of the Bou Inania and Sidi Lazzaz mosques beyond.
w: Everyone's first photograph of Fez, and a colonial fake: the French raised this triple-arched gate in 1913, not some medieval sultan. Cobalt zellij greets you outside, the blue of Fassi pottery; step through and the reverse glows green for Islam. Two minarets line up dead ahead in the arch. A grand entrance, barely a century old.

hook: A square that rings all day with coppersmiths' hammers, beside the city's first madrasa.
facts: Named for the seffarin, the coppersmiths whose workshops ring the square.
Sits south of al-Qarawiyyin near the Bou Khrareb river, beside the Qarawiyyin library.
The Saffarin Madrasa, built 1271, was the first madrasa of the Marinid dynasty.
An old plane tree shades the square where craftsmen still beat trays and cauldrons by hand.
w: Follow the noise: a small square where coppersmiths hammer trays, kettles and vast wedding cauldrons under a leaning plane tree, the din bouncing off the walls all day. On one side stands the Saffarin Madrasa of 1271, the very first the Marinids built. Beaten metal, ancient learning, and an honest racket.

hook: The rope-makers' college, plainer and later than the rest, built to lodge far-flung students.
facts: Begun on 17 December 1670 by the Alawi sultan Moulay al-Rashid.
Named Cherratine for a nearby rope-makers' market.
The largest madrasa of its quarter, it housed students from Algeria, the Rif and the Tafilalt.
Built of brick and cedar around a square courtyard with a marble fountain.
w: Built in 1670 by Moulay al-Rashid, the rope-makers' madrasa skips the riot of zellij for sterner brick and cedar, a working dormitory more than a showpiece. Tiers of tiny cells once packed in students from the Rif, Algeria and the desert south, here to study at al-Qarawiyyin. The plain face of medieval Fez.

hook: The tomb of the city's founder, ringed by wooden bars that once barred outsiders and donkeys.
facts: Shrine and tomb of Idris II, regarded as the main founder of Fez, who ruled 807 to 828.
His body was reportedly found incorrupt around 1308, prompting the shrine's growth.
Largely rebuilt in the 18th century under Moulay Ismail in Alaouite style.
Low wooden bars at the lanes once marked the sacred horm, closed to non-Muslims.
legend: Tradition holds his body was discovered uncorrupted five centuries after his death.
w: The holiest spot in Fez, wrapped so tight in the souk you almost miss it: green-tiled tomb of Idris II, who founded the city around 810. Tradition says his body was found uncorrupted five centuries on. Low wooden bars still cross the lanes, once barring non-Muslims and pack-animals from the sacred ground. Pilgrims press coins to brass.

hook: Founded the same year as al-Qarawiyyin, anchor of the quarter built by refugees from Spain.
facts: Founded 859 to 860 by Maryam al-Fihri, sister of al-Qarawiyyin's founder.
Heart of the district settled by Andalusi migrants from Cordoba, hence its name.
Minaret added in 956; the body largely rebuilt 1203 to 1207 under the Almohads.
Reached by a steep monumental gate; part of the UNESCO-listed medina.
w: Two sisters founded the city's twin great mosques in 859: Fatima built al-Qarawiyyin across the river, Maryam built this one for the exiles from Cordoba who filled the eastern hill. Its tall Almohad gateway crowns a quarter of steep, quieter lanes. Tourists rarely cross over. The other founder's mosque, half-forgotten.

hook: A royal pleasure garden, opened to the city, where old waterwheels still turn beside the river.
facts: Laid out as a royal garden in the 18th to 19th century under the Alaouites.
Opened to the public in 1917; restored and reopened in 2011.
Fed by the re-engineered Bou Khrareb river that also watered the Royal Palace.
Surviving waterwheels stand near its lake of palms, bamboo and old planes.
w: Once a sultan's private retreat between the old and new medinas, thrown open to the city in 1917. Towering palms, a bamboo grove, a still lake, and old wooden waterwheels groaning beside the channelled river. Step in from the dust and noise of the gates and the temperature seems to drop. Royal shade, now everyone's.

hook: A crumbling palace of the Glaoui warlords, still part-lived-in, fading room by gilded room.
facts: Late 19th to early 20th century palace, originally built for the financier Haj Omar Tazi.
Seized in 1907 by the Glaoui family, including Thami, the powerful pasha of Marrakech.
A sprawling complex with as many as twelve houses, hammams, stables and gardens.
Privately owned and partly dilapidated; family members open rooms to visitors.
w: Knock and a member of the family may show you round the wreck of a warlord's palace: peeling painted ceilings, a dust-furred salon, a harem courtyard run to seed. Built for a finance minister, seized in 1907 by the Glaoui clan who half-ruled Morocco for the French. Twelve houses, slowly subsiding. Faded grandeur, lived in still.
